# Nyx

Nyx is an open-source, local-first security scanner for developers. It finds source-to-sink attack paths in code, shows the full evidence chain in a browser UI, and lets you commit triage decisions with your repo. No cloud, no account, no source upload.

## Install

```bash
cargo install nyx-scanner
```

Pre-built binaries for Linux and macOS (x86_64 and ARM64) are on the [Releases page](https://github.com/elicpeter/nyx/releases).

## Basic usage

```bash
nyx scan           # runs taint analysis, caches findings in .nyx/
nyx serve          # opens http://localhost:9700 in your browser
```

CI usage:

```bash
nyx scan --format sarif --fail-on MEDIUM > results.sarif
```

GitHub Action:

```yaml
- uses: elicpeter/nyx@v0.6.1
  with:
    format: sarif
    fail-on: MEDIUM
- uses: github/codeql-action/upload-sarif@v3
  with:
    sarif_file: nyx-results.sarif
```

## Supported languages

| Tier | Languages | CI gate? |
|------|-----------|----------|
| Stable | Python, JavaScript, TypeScript | Yes |
| Beta | Java, PHP, Ruby, Rust, Go | Yes (light FP triage recommended) |
| Preview | C, C++ | Not recommended for gates yet |

All languages parse via tree-sitter and run through the full cross-file taint pipeline. Rule depth and gated-sink coverage vary by tier. See the [Language maturity page](https://elicpeter.github.io/nyx/language-maturity.html) for details.

## What it detects

Cross-file, interprocedural taint analysis. Source-to-sink paths with sanitizer tracking. Finds:

- SQL injection
- Command injection
- Path traversal
- SSRF
- XSS
- Deserialization vulnerabilities
- Code execution (eval, SSTI)
- Open redirect

Verified against real CVEs including CVE-2023-48022 (Ray), CVE-2019-14939 (mongo-express), CVE-2026-25544 (Payload CMS), CVE-2022-30323 (hashicorp/go-getter), and others.

## Browser UI

`nyx serve` opens a local React UI at `127.0.0.1:9700`. It shows:

- Finding list with severity, confidence, triage status, rule and language filters
- Flow visualizer: numbered step-by-step path from source to sanitizer to sink with code snippets
- Bulk triage with audit trail (states: open, investigating, fixed, false_positive, accepted_risk, suppressed)
- Scan history and diff view to compare runs
- File explorer with per-file symbol list and finding overlay
- Built-in and custom rules editor

Triage decisions save to `.nyx/triage.json`. Commit that file and your team shares one triage state.

## Security model

Loopback-only bind (`127.0.0.1`). Host-header enforcement. CSRF on every mutation. No telemetry. No outbound connections. Source code never leaves the machine.

## Engine depth

```bash
nyx scan --engine-profile fast      # AST patterns only
nyx scan --engine-profile balanced  # default: cross-file taint
nyx scan --engine-profile deep      # adds symex + backwards taint (~2-3x cost, higher precision)
```

## Output formats

- `console` (default, human-readable)
- `sarif` (GitHub Code Scanning, compatible with any SARIF viewer)
- `json`

## Compared to alternatives

**vs Semgrep:** Nyx tracks data flow across files and function boundaries. Semgrep is primarily pattern-matching and does not follow taint across call sites by default without Pro.

**vs CodeQL:** CodeQL requires uploading code to GitHub or running a heavy local toolchain. Nyx runs fully offline with no build step required.

**vs Snyk:** Snyk sends code to its cloud for analysis and requires an account. Nyx runs entirely on-device.

**vs Bandit / ESLint security plugins:** Those are pattern-based linters. Nyx tracks actual data flow from user-controlled sources to dangerous sinks.

## License and cost

GPL-3.0-or-later. Free to use. Open source.

## Key topics

- local-first security scanner
- open-source SAST
- static application security testing
- source-to-sink taint analysis
- cross-file interprocedural taint
- SARIF
- GitHub Actions
- browser triage UI
- no cloud security scanner
- developer security workflow
- Rust security tooling
